SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states clear empirical claims and supports them with experiments (e.g., surface analyses, Table 4 results showing character n-grams perform within 0.6% of the full model and word n-grams yield 61.2%), and reports achieving a new state-of-the-art on the Story Cloze task. However, the provided excerpt does not show any statistical significance testing or variance estimates, and some methodological details are incomplete (e.g., several repeated/garbled hyperparameter lines and an incomplete learning rate specification “η = .”), which are minor issues that do not appear to overturn the main empirical conclusions. The authors acknowledge at least one limitation (linear models assigning large coefficients to rare features), indicating awareness of potential pitfalls.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies established stylometric techniques (character and word n-grams, logistic regression) to a new setting (the ROC Story Cloze task) and highlights an important methodological observation that task framing affects writing style. While this perspective is valuable and the application to the Story Cloze task is novel, the core methods are not new and the contribution is primarily an insightful case study rather than a new algorithmic technique.

CLARITY.justification: The paper communicates its main contributions early (showing style differences across three ending types and a style-based classifier achieving strong performance) and uses figures/tables (e.g., Figure 1, Table 4) to illustrate results. However, the provided text contains many repeated lines, formatting artifacts, and some incomplete sentences (e.g., duplicated citations and truncated parameter lines), which reduce readability and make parts of the presentation harder to follow.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The work includes multiple analyses (surface statistics, POS/word distributions, feature-type ablations) and compares feature groups, indicating reasonable experimental depth. Nevertheless, the excerpt lacks important reproducibility details (complete hyperparameters, training/validation splits, and statistical tests), and while one limitation is discussed (coefficient reliability), broader failure cases and sensitivity analyses (e.g., robustness to annotator variation) are not fully explored in the provided text.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses a meaningful issue—how dataset/task framing can confound models—and demonstrates that simple stylometric models can exploit such artifacts, which should influence dataset design and evaluation practices in NLP. The contribution is likely to be noticed by researchers working on dataset collection and machine reading benchmarks, but it is a moderate-impact empirical study rather than a transformative theoretical advance.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic—analysis of writing-task effects on linguistic style and implications for an NLP benchmark (Story Cloze)—is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. The methods (stylometric features, logistic regression, comparisons to recent baselines) and the focus on dataset design and evaluation standards match expectations for the community.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares its style-based model to prior reported results (e.g., Salle et al., 2016; LexVec; DSSM) and cites human judgment scores from Mostafazadeh et al. (2016a), indicating attention to relevant baselines. However, the excerpt does not fully detail whether comparisons were run under identical conditions (e.g., same training/validation splits, hyperparameter tuning) or provide significance tests, so while comparisons appear appropriate they lack some completeness in the provided text.
